findings.title: validate_distillation_safety checks for forbidden patterns
findings.description: The function checks for: decimal numbers with 2+ decimal places, buy/sell/做多/做空 keywords (Chinese and English), confidence numbers (置信度, confidence). These patterns would indicate raw trading signals or price levels leaking into methodology prompts. The check is applied before methodology injection text is used.

findings.title: Denylist approach has inherent coverage gaps
findings.description: validate_distillation_safety() uses regex denylist. It does NOT check for: role-playing prompts ('you are now...'), instruction injection ('从今以后', 'your new task is...'), escape sequences, or prompt delimiter injection ('---', '==='). However, the data SOURCE is controlled: recurring_patterns contains only ROOT_CAUSE_TAXONOMY keys (hardcoded strings like 'MISSING_DATA', 'FLAWED_CHAIN'), and common_blind_spots contains only taxonomy keys. So the actual injection surface through these fields is zero — taxonomy keys cannot contain arbitrary text.
findings.remediation: LOW priority. If future versions expand recurring_patterns to include LLM-generated text (e.g., extracted patterns from analysis), the denylist should be upgraded to an allowlist or structural validation. Current risk is minimal given the controlled data source.

findings.title: generate_methodology_injection output contains static disclaimer
findings.description: Line 89-91: '仅供方法论参考，不构成投资建议' (for methodology reference only, does not constitute investment advice). This is injected into every methodology prompt. Good practice for compliance.

findings.title: No LLM calls, no I/O — no direct injection surface
findings.description: The module performs pure computation on data passed in as parameters. No LLM calls, no file I/O, no network access. The output (methodology injection strings) is consumed by downstream prompt construction, which is protected by gateway-level input_guard. The security boundary is at the gateway, not at this module.

findings.title: Shadow ID parsed from tracker_id string splitting
findings.description: Line 53: shadow_id = tracker_id.split('_')[1]. If tracker_id format is 'shadow_X_entity' and X contains underscores, this split would produce wrong results. Not a security issue — just a parsing fragility that could attribute expertise to the wrong shadow.
findings.remediation: LOW priority. Consider using a more robust parsing method (e.g., regex with named groups) or storing shadow_id explicitly in calibration_data.

notes: The denylist-based safety validation is adequate given that the data source is controlled (taxonomy keys only). The module is a pure computation leaf with no I/O surface. The security boundary for prompt injection is correctly placed at the gateway level.
